content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Anthony Fortenberry , 
PMB 140 
910 Pleasant Grove Blvd, Ste 120 
Roseville, CA 95768 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-18-132 
Dear Mr. Fortenberry: 
August 24, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental employment 
provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act"). 1 Because your inquiry is general in nature and does 
not involve a proceeding before a state agency, we are treating your request as one for informal 
assistance. 2 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the post-governmental employment 
provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post-
government employment laws, such as Public Contract Code Section 10411. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
As a former state agency employee with the Office of Systems Integration ("OSI") do the 
Act's revolving door provisions preclude you from consulting on the California Department of 
Health Care Services ("CDHCS") Medical Management Information Replacement Project, with the 
California Health and Human Services ("CHHS"), or with the California Department of 
Technology ("CDT")? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-18-132 
Page No. 2 
From the facts you have provided, the Act's one-year ban prohibits you from appearing, or 
making a communication before the Office of Systems Integration ("OSI") for compensation and 
for the purpose of influencing an administrative or legislative action or any action or proceeding 
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, 
of the sale or purchase of goods or property. The one-year ban does not restrict your appearances 
before the CDHCS, CHHS, or the CDT. However, the Act's permanent ban prohibits you from 
participating in certain proceedings involving the State of California or assisting others in the 
proceedings, if you previously participated in the proceedings while employed by the state. 
FACTS AS PRESTENED BY REQUESTOR 
You were employed by the OSI from August 1, 2016 through May 18, 2018 as the OSI 
Project Director (August 1, 2016-August 1, 2017), Deputy Director (August 2, 2017-March 17, 
2018), and Digital Services Director (March 18, 2018-May 18, 2018) for OSI's California Welfare 
Digital Services ("CWDS") project entitled Child Welfare Services-California Automated 
Response and Engagement System ("CWS-CARES"). 
The OSI was established to manage a portfolio of large, complex health and human services 
information technology projects. OSI controls its own budget, its personnel management is 
independent of the CHHS, and it has independent operational controls including its own human 
resources, administration, and information technology departments. The office provides project 
management, oversight, procurement and support services for a multi-billion dollar portfolio of 
highly critical projects. In this capacity, OSI coordinates communication, collaboration, and 
decision-making among project stakeholders and program-side sponsors of the projects. OSI 
manages the procurement, contract negotiations, and contract management aspects of the 
acquisition of technology systems and services. After the procurement phase, OSI oversees the 
design, development, governance, and implementation of information technology systems that serve 
health and human services programs. Since its inception, OSI has developed a track record of 
successfully managing and deploying large, complex, mission critical systems to support health and 
human services programs at the state, federal and local level. 3 
In your position at OSI, you exclusively worked on the CWS-CARES project. Under your 
assignment duties within the CWS-CARES project, you contributed to Procurement and contract 
management activity at CWDS. CWDS is a collaboration of state and local government agencies 
dedicated to building a new child welfare information system that will replace the legacy case 
management system in operation since the l 990s. Your involvement with CWDS also entailed 
review and/or approval of all vendor contracts and invoices. 
You did not formally work for CHHS and had no formal assignment nor authority in any 
other project other than OSI's CWS-CARES project. You did not evaluate or approve contracts for 
other OSI projects, OSI headquarters, other departments, or at the CHHS agency level. Other than 
3 Background infonnation about OSI can be located using the following hyperlink: 
http://www.osi.oa.gov/ A boutUs.shtml 


File No. A-18-132 
Page No. 3 
ongoing individual interaction with OSI leadership, you had no permanent role or duties regarding 
central OSI operations. 
For OSI, you attended bi-weekly "executive meetings," which included the various project 
directors, bi-weekly "leadership meetings," which included the OSI Deputy Directors, and other 
OSI special events, such as "town hall meetings." These meetings were populated with peers 
throughout the Department of Social Services, the Department of Health Care Services, OSI, 
CWDS, and CHHS. CWS-CARES was an innovative project and a topic of interest. In the spirit of 
knowledge sharing and collaboration, you all contributed to many discussions with other state 
managers and technical teams. 
· 
You separated from state employment on May 18, 2018 and are currently employed as a 
consultant with CivicActions, Inc., a digital services firm providing government services. 
Specifically, CivicActions provides services to CWDS and the CDHCS Medical Management 
Information Replacement Project. CivicActions, Inc. may also assign you to consult and train 
officials at the CHHS or CDT. You do not intend to participate in providing services to CWDS 
under any contract that was active during your tenure with OSI. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. These provisions 
are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions. 
One-Year Ban: 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her 
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406; Regulation 
18746.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
(Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746. l(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date 
the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban 
applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or 
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or 
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(3) and (4).) 
In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to certain matters involving specific 
parties such as ''judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appear~nce 
or communication made to influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action 
or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 187 46.1 (b )( 5).) An 
appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the "principal 


File No. A-18-132 
Page No. 4 
purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the 
action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication includes, but is not 
limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic 
transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Id.) 
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (1) before a state 
agency that the public official worked for or represented, (2) before a state agency "which budget, 
personn~l, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public official 
worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction and co·ntrol of the 
Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office during the twelve 
months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(6)(C).) 
As a decision-making state administrative official at OSI, you were a designated employee 
as defined in Section 82019. Therefore, for one year after leaving OSI, you may not communicate 
with your former agency in an attempt to influence any transaction involving legislative or 
administrative action4 or other discretionary act - including contracts. 5 
For purposes of identifying which state agency a former employee has worked for or 
represented, we have previously made the following determination: 
"In identifying an official's 'state administrative agency,' within the meaning of 
[S]ection 87406, we look to the administrative body that controls the budget, 
personnel and other operations of the entity where a former employee worked. It 
is this body, together with any agencies, boards, commissions, or organizational 
units subject to its control, that are jointly considered to be the ex-employee's 
former state administrative agency employer." (Corum Advice Letter, No. A-02-
258.) 
4 For purposes of Section 87406, the Act defines "administrative action" as the following: "'Administrative 
action' means the proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency 
of any rule, regulation, or other action in any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding .... " (Section 
82002(a).) "Legislative action" is defined as the "drafting, introduction, consideration, modification, enactment or 
defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by either house or 
any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting 
in his official capacity." (Section 82037) 
5 Not all communications to a former state administrative agency employer are prohibited by the one-year ban. 
The ban extends only to those communications made for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative 
action, or influencing any discretionary act "involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, 
license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Section 87406(d)(l ); Regulation 
18746.1 (b )(5).) Communications restricted by the one-year ban include any formal or informal appearance or oral or 
written communication made to influence legislative or administrative action or any action on a proceeding. (Section 
87406(d)(l).) These communications include, but are not limited to, conversing directly or by telephone, corresponding 
by writing or e-mail, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Regulation 18746.2(a).) A 
communication is considered to be for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action "if it is made for 
the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or 
proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2(a).) 


File No. A-18-132 
Page No. 5 
As a former employee of OSI, the one-year ban will apply to any appearances or 
communications you may make before or with OSI. However, we have advised that a former 
agency official may, without violating the one-year ban, draft proposals on a client's behalf to be 
submitted to the agency so long as the former employee is not identified in connection with.the 
client's efforts to influence an administrative action. (Cook Advice Letter, No. A-95-321; Harrison 
Advice Letter, No. A-92-289.) Similarly, a former agency official may use his or her expertise to 
advise clients on the procedural requirements, plans, or policies of the official's former agency so 
long as the employee is not identified with the employer's efforts to influence the agency. (Perry 
Advice Letter, No. A-94-004.) 
Turning to the question of whether you previously worked for or represented the CHHS, 
OSI is within the organizational structure of CHHS. However, you have stated that the OSI has and 
controls its own budget, its personnel management is independent of the CHHS, and it has 
independent operational controls. Based upon these facts, it appears that the OSI is a separate state 
agency than the CHHS for purposes of the Act's revolving door provisions, and the one-year ban 
would not restrict your appearances or communications with the CHHS. (See Bayquen Advice 
Letter, No. I-09-230.) You also did not work for the CDHCS or the CDT, and you have not 
provided any facts that the budgets, personnel or operations of either department are within the 
direction or control of OSI. Thus, barring additional facts, the one-year ban does not apply to your 
appearances or communications before CDHCS, CHHS, or CDT. 
Permanent Ban: 
The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in a certain proceeding involving the State of California and other 
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state 
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401' and 87402; Regulation 
18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of 
absence from any office or employment. (Regulation 18741.l(a)(l).) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication- or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, 6 in an appearance or 
communication - made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
'" Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request 
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency ... " (Section 87400( c ).) Thus, the permanent ban covers proceedings that 
affect the rights or claims of specific parties. (Lowry Advice Letter, No. 1-08-053.) It does not apply 
to those that involve the making of rules or policies of general applicability. (Beale Advice Letter, 
No. A-00-146.) 
6 The state encompasses all California state agencies, including all divisions of the CPUC. (Section 87401.) 
The permanent ban is potentially applicable if you represent any other entity than the State of California. 


File No. A-18-132 
Page No. 6 
Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she 
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, 
formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of 
confidential information ... " (Section 87400(d).) 
Among your decision-making responsibilities at OSI, you reviewed, managed, and made 
decisions to approve or disapprove various contracts involving the agency's vendors. You have 
been involved in contractual matters with CWDS or other specific parties before a state agency. 
Therefore, these contracts with vendors are considered a "proceeding" under the Act's permanent 
ban on switching sides in a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding to which the permanent ban 
will apply under Sections 87400 and 87401. (See Walter Advice Letter, No. I-06-078.) 
Accordingly, you may not represent or advise any other party on contracts you have participated in 
while employed in state service. (See Regulation 18741.l(a)(4).)7 
However, the Act would not prohibit you from working on proceedings, such as the CDHCS 
Medical Management Information Replacement Project or from consulting and training officials at 
the CHHS or CDT so long as you have not previously participated in these proceedings. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
RPOC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
~ 7:e>~ 
/4yan P. O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division 
7 We note that you have expressed that you do not intend to participate in providing services to CWDS under 
any contract that was active during your tenure as OSI.

written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).)

are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions.

grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406; Regulation 
18746.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
(Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746. l(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date 
the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban

representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(3) and (4).) 
In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to certain matters involving specific

or communication made to influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action 
or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,

appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the "principal  
purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the

former state administrative agency employer." (Corum Advice Letter, No. A-02-
258.) 
4 For purposes of Section 87406, the Act defines "administrative action" as the following: "'Administrative

of any rule, regulation, or other action in any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding .... " (Section

defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by either house or

in his official capacity." (Section 82037)

The ban extends only to those communications made for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative

communications you may make before or with OSI. However, we have advised that a former

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication- or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, 6 in an appearance or 
communication - made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
'" Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request 
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency ... " (Section 87400( c ).) Thus, the permanent ban covers proceedings that 
affect the rights or claims of specific parties. (Lowry Advice Letter, No. 1-08-053.) It does not apply 
to those that involve the making of rules or policies of general applicability. (Beale Advice Letter, 
No. A-00-146.) 
6 The state encompasses all California state agencies, including all divisions of the CPUC. (Section 87401.) 
The permanent ban is potentially applicable if you represent any other entity than the State of California.  
Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she 
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, 
formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of

been involved in contractual matters with CWDS or other specific parties before a state agency. 
Therefore, these contracts with vendors are considered a "proceeding" under the Act's permanent 
ban on switching sides in a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding to which the permanent ban 
will apply under Sections 87400 and 87401. (See Walter Advice Letter, No. I-06-078.) 
Accordingly, you may not represent or advise any other party on contracts you have participated in 
while employed in state service. (See Regulation 18741.l(a)(4).)7 
However, the Act would not prohibit you from working on proceedings, such as the CDHCS 
Medical Management Information Replacement Project or from consulting and training officials at 
the CHHS or CDT so long as you have not previously participated in these proceedings.

Act's revolving door provisions preclude you from consulting on the California Department of 
Health Care Services ("CDHCS") Medical Management Information Replacement Project, with the 
California Health and Human Services ("CHHS"), or with the California Department of 
Technology ("CDT")?2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).)

CONCLUSION: From the facts you have provided, the Act's one-year ban prohibits you from appearing, or 
making a communication before the Office of Systems Integration ("OSI") for compensation and 
for the purpose of influencing an administrative or legislative action or any action or proceeding 
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, 
of the sale or purchase of goods or property. The one-year ban does not restrict your appearances 
before the CDHCS, CHHS, or the CDT. However, the Act's permanent ban prohibits you from 
participating in certain proceedings involving the State of California or assisting others in the 
proceedings, if you previously participated in the proceedings while employed by the state. 
FACTS AS PRESTENED BY REQUESTOR 
You were employed by the OSI from August 1, 2016 through May 18, 2018 as the OSI 
Project Director (August 1, 2016-August 1, 2017), Deputy Director (August 2, 2017-March 17, 
2018), and Digital Services Director (March 18, 2018-May 18, 2018) for OSI's California Welfare 
Digital Services ("CWDS") project entitled Child Welfare Services-California Automated 
Response and Engagement System ("CWS-CARES"). 
The OSI was established to manage a portfolio of large, complex health and human services 
information technology projects. OSI controls its own budget, its personnel management is 
independent of the CHHS, and it has independent operational controls including its own human 
resources, administration, and information technology departments. The office provides project 
management, oversight, procurement and support services for a multi-billion dollar portfolio of 
highly critical projects. In this capacity, OSI coordinates communication, collaboration, and 
decision-making among project stakeholders and program-side sponsors of the projects. OSI 
manages the procurement, contract negotiations, and contract management aspects of the 
acquisition of technology systems and services. After the procurement phase, OSI oversees the 
design, development, governance, and implementation of information technology systems that serve 
health and human services programs. Since its inception, OSI has developed a track record of 
successfully managing and deploying large, complex, mission critical systems to support health and 
human services programs at the state, federal and local level. 3 
In your position at OSI, you exclusively worked on the CWS-CARES project. Under your 
assignment duties within the CWS-CARES project, you contributed to Procurement and contract 
management activity at CWDS. CWDS is a collaboration of state and local government agencies 
dedicated to building a new child welfare information system that will replace the legacy case 
management system in operation since the l 990s. Your involvement with CWDS also entailed 
review and/or approval of all vendor contracts and invoices. 
You did not formally work for CHHS and had no formal assignment nor authority in any 
other project other than OSI's CWS-CARES project. You did not evaluate or approve contracts for 
other OSI projects, OSI headquarters, other departments, or at the CHHS agency level. Other than 
3 Background infonnation about OSI can be located using the following hyperlink: 
http://www.osi.oa.gov/ A boutUs.shtml  
ongoing individual interaction with OSI leadership, you had no permanent role or duties regarding 
central OSI operations. 
For OSI, you attended bi-weekly "executive meetings," which included the various project 
directors, bi-weekly "leadership meetings," which included the OSI Deputy Directors, and other 
OSI special events, such as "town hall meetings." These meetings were populated with peers 
throughout the Department of Social Services, the Department of Health Care Services, OSI, 
CWDS, and CHHS. CWS-CARES was an innovative project and a topic of interest. In the spirit of 
knowledge sharing and collaboration, you all contributed to many discussions with other state 
managers and technical teams. 
· 
You separated from state employment on May 18, 2018 and are currently employed as a 
consultant with CivicActions, Inc., a digital services firm providing government services. 
Specifically, CivicActions provides services to CWDS and the CDHCS Medical Management 
Information Replacement Project. CivicActions, Inc. may also assign you to consult and train 
officials at the CHHS or CDT. You do not intend to participate in providing services to CWDS 
under any contract that was active during your tenure with OSI.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION • 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Anthony Fortenberry , PMB 140 910 Pleasant Grove Blvd, Ste 120 Roseville, CA 95768 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. 1-18-132 Dear Mr. Fortenberry: August 24, 2018 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental employment provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act"). 1 Because your inquiry is general in nature and does not involve a proceeding before a state agency, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. 2 Please note that we are only providing advice under the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post- government employment laws, such as Public Contract Code Section 10411. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTION As a former state agency employee with the Office of Systems Integration ("OSI") do the Act's revolving door provisions preclude you from consulting on the California Department of Health Care Services ("CDHCS") Medical Management Information Replacement Project, with the California Health and Human Services ("CHHS"), or with the California Department of Technology ("CDT")? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) CONCLUSION File No. A-18-132 Page No. 2 From the facts you have provided, the Act's one-year ban prohibits you from appearing, or making a communication before the Office of Systems Integration ("OSI") for compensation and for the purpose of influencing an administrative or legislative action or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, of the sale or purchase of goods or property. The one-year ban does not restrict your appearances before the CDHCS, CHHS, or the CDT. However, the Act's permanent ban prohibits you from participating in certain proceedings involving the State of California or assisting others in the proceedings, if you previously participated in the proceedings while employed by the state. FACTS AS PRESTENED BY REQUESTOR You were employed by the OSI from August 1, 2016 through May 18, 2018 as the OSI Project Director (August 1, 2016-August 1, 2017), Deputy Director (August 2, 2017-March 17, 2018), and Digital Services
